Netflix Earnings
----------------

From the Wall Street Journal ( https://www.wsj.com/articles/netflix-nflx-q2=
-earnings-report-2023-92a620c8 ) :

Netflix=E2=80=99s global password-sharing crackdown delivered robust subscr=
iber growth in the second quarter, a boon for the company as its rivals str=
uggle with flagging TV businesses and costly pivots to streaming. The compa=
ny gained 5.9 million subscribers, after losing nearly one million customer=
s in the year-ago quarter, as people who are no longer able to share the se=
rvice for free opted to pay for their own accounts. The company said it is =
now rolling out that effort in nearly every remaining country.

The continuing Hollywood writers and actors strikes have led Netflix to spe=
nd less on content this year, the company said. It raised its free cash flo=
w forecast to $5 billion from an earlier estimate of $3.5 billion=E2=80=A6R=
evenue fell short of the company=E2=80=99s projections, weighed down in par=
t by price cuts overseas earlier this year, growth in markets with lower av=
erage revenue per user, and adding new customers late in the quarter. Share=
s fell 8.5% in aftermarket trading; before the earnings report, the stock w=
as up more than 60% in 2023.

I=E2=80=99m not sure there is enough evidence to support the framing around=
 Netflix=E2=80=99s password crackdown. The streaming service=E2=80=99s word=
ing around its subscriber additions was very carefully phrased in its Share=
holder Letter ( https://s22.q4cdn.com/959853165/files/doc_financials/2023/q=
2/FINAL-Q2-23-Shareholder-Letter.pdf ). First, right at the top (formatting=
 copied from the letter):

* In May, we successfully launched paid sharing in 100+ countries, represen=
ting more than 80% of our revenue base.

* Revenue in each region is now higher than pre-launch, with sign-ups alrea=
dy exceeding cancellations.
* Paid net additions were 5.9M in Q2, and today we=E2=80=99re rolling out p=
aid sharing to almost all of the remaining countries.

Then, in the =E2=80=9CQ2 Results=E2=80=9D section:

We had paid net additions of 5.9M for Q2=E2=80=9923 (with over 1M paid net =
adds in each region) vs. -1.0M in Q2=E2=80=9922 as we successfully rolled o=
ut paid sharing to more than 100 countries (representing over 80% of our re=
venue).

Notice that Netflix is careful to state that it added 5.9 million net new s=
ubscribers in the same quarter as it rolled out paid sharing; the company d=
oes not explicitly say that paid sharing drove new subscribers, although it=
 certainly seems to hope that that is the takeaway (and it largely was). I=
=E2=80=99m not saying it=E2=80=99s not true, but I did find the lack of an =
explicit tie by Netflix management interesting.

Another thing that is interesting is that while some people who were previo=
usly sharing passwords may sign up for a new account, accounts that previou=
sly shared passwords can also purchase =E2=80=9Cextra member slots=E2=80=9D=
 for $7.99 each/month. These =E2=80=9Cextra member slots=E2=80=9D are not c=
ounted as new subscribers; footnote 3 in the shareholder letter states:

As a reminder, extra member accounts are not included in our paid membershi=
p count but add revenue that is included in ARM.

This footnote appeared after a sentence where Netflix said:

Revenue from advertising and our extra member feature are not yet material =
enough to offset these factors.

To build on the previous point, it does seem odd to me to realize that =E2=
=80=9Cextra slots=E2=80=9D have not driven material revenue, while simultan=
eously crediting the crackdown on password sharing to Netflix=E2=80=99s sub=
scriber growth; was the vast majority of password sharing just a matter of =
convenience =E2=80=94 i.e. friends giving out their passwords =E2=80=94 and=
 not much a matter of, say, parents sharing a password with their child, in=
 which case they might be more willing to pay? This is definitely possible;=
 regardless, I would like to see more explicit evidence to support the Wall=
 Street Journal=E2=80=99s contention that =E2=80=9CNetflix=E2=80=99s global=
 password-sharing crackdown delivered robust subscriber growth=E2=80=9D.

For the record, I think the framing is probably correct, just as I think th=
at Netflix=E2=80=99s crackdown is reasonable; it never fully made sense to =
me that people paying for Netflix would suddenly stop because free-loaders =
were cut off. Still, I think the jury is still out.

What is clear is that Netflix=E2=80=99s average revenue per member (ARM) co=
ntinues to drop, not just globally, but also in the United States and Canad=
a. This is notable because one of the big questions around the company=E2=
=80=99s addition of advertising was whether or not it would cannibalize the=
 company=E2=80=99s ARM. This, I would note, would not be fatal: more subscr=
ibers at a lower ARM can be revenue accretive, and, given the nature of the=
 company=E2=80=99s costs, good for margins.

This last point is worth highlighting; Netflix=E2=80=99s operating margin w=
as up despite that decreasing ARM figure, because the company=E2=80=99s cos=
ts are primarily fixed costs (around content and below-the-line expenses), =
not marginal costs. This means that more members increases margins, because=
 those fixed costs can be spread across that many more members; this is als=
o in contrast to companies with significant marginal costs, which would see=
 margins drop as ARM drops.

The first step in increasing advertising scale is (1) making the advertisin=
